item.title: Russian SMS Phishing Targets Messaging Accounts; Clean GitHub Repos Abuse Claude Code
item.content:encoded:
Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials — Must Read

Ukraine’s SSU says Russian intelligence ran a long-running phishing campaign using fake messaging support texts to steal credentials from officials, military personnel, politicians, activists, and other users in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S. Users are urged to review active sessions, enable two-factor authentication, and never share codes, passwords, recovery keys, or scan suspicious QR codes.

  • The Security Service of Ukraine said it and the FBI uncovered a long-running Russian intelligence campaign targeting messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, activists, and other users in Ukraine, Europe, and the United States.
  • The attackers sent SMS messages pretending to be from a messaging platform support bot to persuade users to disclose their account credentials.
  • The SSU said the campaign sought access to sensitive military, political, and economic information, as well as victims’ personal data.
  • The SSU did not attribute the campaign to a specific hacking group, but the article notes that similar attacks against Signal and WhatsApp users have been linked to Star Blizzard, UNC5792, and UNC4221.
  • Recommended precautions include reviewing active messaging app sessions, logging out of unknown connections, enabling two-factor authentication, avoiding unknown QR codes, and not sharing confirmation codes, PINs, passwords, or recovery keys.

Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware — Should Read

Mozilla 0DIN researchers showed that an apparently clean GitHub repository can trick an AI coding agent like Claude Code into running setup commands that fetch and execute an attacker-controlled payload from a DNS TXT record. The key risk is that agents may automatically “fix” normal-looking setup errors and execute hidden command chains that give attackers a shell with the developer’s privileges.

  • Mozilla's 0DIN researchers demonstrated an attack concept in which Claude Code could be tricked into running a malicious payload from a GitHub repository that contains no malicious code.
  • The method uses a clean-looking repository, a Python package that asks to be initialized after failing to run, and an initialization command that executes a shell script retrieving a command from an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record.
  • 0DIN said Claude Code treats the package error as a normal setup issue and automatically runs the suggested initialization command while attempting to recover.
  • If successful, the attacker would obtain an interactive shell running with the developer’s privileges, potentially exposing environment variables, API keys, local configuration files, and enabling persistence.
  • 0DIN warned that threat actors could distribute such repositories through fake job postings, tutorials, blog posts, or direct messages.

item.title: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol Preview and Healthcare Invoice Phishing via SaaS Redirects
item.content:encoded:
OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards — Should Read

OpenAI is previewing GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna to a small group of government-approved partners, with Sol positioned as its most capable cybersecurity model yet. The company says stronger safeguards are intended to support legitimate defensive work while blocking offensive cyber misuse ahead of a broader release.

  • OpenAI released three versions of GPT-5.6, named Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to a small number of companies as part of an ongoing engagement with the U.S. government.
  • OpenAI described GPT-5.6 Sol as its latest flagship and most powerful model, with its strongest safety stack to date for higher-risk activity, sensitive cyber requests, and repeated misuse.
  • OpenAI said GPT-5.6 Sol is its most capable model yet for cybersecurity and is intended to support legitimate work such as code review, vulnerability research, patch development, debugging, security education, and defensive testing.
  • OpenAI warned that users in the preview phase may encounter safeguards that block or refuse legitimate requests or pause requests for additional review because of the dual-use nature of the technology.
  • OpenAI said GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna are planned for general availability in the coming weeks after previewing the model capabilities to the U.S. government and approved trusted partners.

Forged Healthcare Sender, Two-Hop SaaS Redirect: How a Fake Invoice Opens a Phishing Chain — May Read

Attackers spoofed a long-established healthcare provider and used legitimate SaaS redirect services to hide a phishing link behind an “Open document” button. The key warning signs were failed DKIM/DMARC authentication, no prior sender relationship, and a link chain unrelated to the claimed sender or document-sharing workflow.

  • Attackers forged the From address of a healthcare provider whose domain was registered in October 1995, causing DKIM to fail and DMARC to apply a quarantine policy.
  • The email used an invoice-themed subject and a green “Open document” button claiming access to “DOC - 19017.pdf,” but it contained no attachment or actual document.
  • The button linked first to holabrief.com, a legitimate creative-brief collaboration platform, and the email HTML also contained a saferedirecturl attribute pointing to loopedin.io as a fallback redirect path.
  • The article says neither HolaBrief nor LoopedIn was malicious, but both legitimate SaaS services were abused as redirect infrastructure to place trusted hostnames in the link chain.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 82% confidence as “Invoice Phishing” based on the combination of authentication failure, first-contact sender status, and a link chain inconsistent with the claimed healthcare sender.

item.title: Cyera Finds Exposed Agentic AI Credentials; FBI Warns UNC5792 Targets Signal Backup Keys
item.content:encoded:
FBI: Russian hackers now target Signal backup recovery keys — Must Read

The FBI and CISA warn that Russian intelligence-linked phishing campaigns are now tricking high-value Signal users into revealing Backup Recovery Keys, which lets attackers restore and read their historical messages. Users should never share Signal recovery keys and must generate a new key if one may have been exposed.

  • The FBI and CISA warned that a phishing campaign tied to Russian intelligence services has evolved to steal Signal Backup Recovery Keys, which can allow attackers to access victims’ historical messages.
  • The campaign targets individuals of high intelligence value, including current and former US and international government officials, military personnel, political figures, journalists, and key officials located in Ukraine.
  • The FBI attributes the activity to Russian Intelligence Services, including officers embedded with Russia’s Federal Security Service Border Guards and other actors working on behalf of the Russian military, and says the campaign is publicly tracked as UNC5792 and UNC4221.
  • The attackers impersonate Signal support and instruct victims to enable Signal backups, copy the recovery key, and later paste it into a message under the false claim that their data is at risk of loss due to a synchronization issue.
  • The FBI warned that creating a new Signal account with the same phone number does not invalidate a stolen Backup Recovery Key, and users must generate a new key in Signal’s backup settings to invalidate the previous key for future backup downloads.

The Hidden Attack Surface of Agentic AI: Securing AI Agent Integration Platforms — Should Read

Cyera found widespread exposure of API keys for AI agent integration platforms like Composio, Nango, Arcade, and others, showing that leaked credentials can unlock broad access to connected tools such as email, code repositories, CI/CD systems, and enterprise apps. The key takeaway is that organizations need to inventory and govern their AI integration layer because a single exposed agent-platform key can create a large downstream breach path.

  • Cyera researchers found thousands of exposed credentials for AI integration platforms including Composio, Arcade, Nango, Tavily, Exa, LlamaIndex, Firecrawl, and other services across hundreds of organizations.
  • The article says AI integration platforms can become highly privileged points in an agent stack because a single integration may grant access to many downstream applications and services.
  • In Cyera’s Composio test, a read-only call returned a Gmail access token and refresh token, a GitHub bearer token, and a CircleCI API key from connected tools.
  • The article states that rotating a leaked Composio key may not end an incident because downstream refresh tokens can continue working until each connected service grant is revoked.
  • Cyera recommends that organizations build a continuously updated inventory of MCP servers, agent governance platforms, retrieval services, and integration providers, including unsanctioned and externally facing deployments.

item.title: CISA Adds CVE-2026-20230 and CVE-2026-12569; Russian Signal, SharkLoader, Polymarket Incidents
item.content:encoded:
CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks — Must Read

CISA ordered U.S. federal agencies to urgently patch an actively exploited critical Cisco Unified Communications Manager SSRF flaw, CVE-2026-20230, by June 28. The agency also set the same deadline for a critical PTC Windchill and FlexPLM remote code execution flaw, CVE-2026-12569.

  • CISA added CVE-2026-20230, a server-side request forgery vulnerability in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after active exploitation was reported.
  • Federal agencies have until Sunday, June 28, under Binding Operational Directive 26-04 to remediate CVE-2026-20230.
  • Cisco rated CVE-2026-20230 as critical and released a patch on June 3, warning that it could be exploited remotely without authentication through specially crafted HTTP requests.
  • CISA also added CVE-2026-12569, a critical remote code execution flaw affecting PTC Windchill and FlexPLM, to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with the same June 28 remediation deadline.
  • PTC disclosed CVE-2026-12569 on June 18 and said it affects all versions up to 11.0 and multiple versions of the 11.1, 11.2, 12.0, 12.1, and 13.0 release branches.

FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys — Must Read

The FBI and CISA warn that Russian intelligence-linked hackers are phishing high-value targets into revealing Signal Backup Recovery Keys, letting attackers restore message archives and potentially take over accounts. Signal users should never share recovery keys, codes, or PINs in chats and should regenerate the key immediately if it may have been exposed.

  • The FBI and CISA updated their March warning to say Russian intelligence-linked operators are now trying to coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup Recovery Key.
  • If attackers obtain a Signal Backup Recovery Key, they can restore the account’s backup, read private and group message history, and take over the account.
  • The updated advisory identifies the activity with the public tracking names UNC5792 and UNC4221 and ties it to multiple Russian Intelligence Services groups.
  • The campaign targets high-intelligence-value individuals, including current and former U.S. and international government officials, military personnel, political figures, journalists, and officials in Ukraine.
  • The agencies state that the activity does not break Signal’s encryption or the app itself, but instead compromises individual accounts through social engineering and legitimate features.

New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks — Should Read

Kaspersky reports a broad, likely Chinese-speaking campaign called StrikeShark that uses a new SharkLoader malware to deploy Cobalt Strike after exploiting public-facing server vulnerabilities or using malicious installers. Targets include diplomatic, government, and software development organizations across multiple countries, suggesting possible espionage despite no confirmed data exfiltration yet.

  • Kaspersky is tracking a newly discovered campaign called StrikeShark that delivers a previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader to deploy Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised hosts.
  • The campaign has targeted a diplomatic organization in Indonesia, government organizations in Taiwan, software development companies across multiple countries, and entities in Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, and Serbia.
  • The attackers have used known vulnerabilities in public-facing applications, including flaws in Exchange Server, Openfire, GeoServer, Apache Shiro, Hikvision products, Microsoft SharePoint, Zimbra Collaboration Suite, F5 BIG-IP, Fortinet FortiOS, React Server Components, and Cisco IOS XE Web UI.
  • SharkLoader is delivered through web shells that trigger a DLL side-loading chain involving SystemSettings.exe, as well as through custom dropper executables disguised as legitimate installers or applications such as Google Update and Cisco AnyConnect.
  • After compromise, the threat actor performs reconnaissance, Active Directory enumeration, credential theft targeting LSASS and the NTDS database file, and deployment of open-source tools such as FScan, Searchall, and Pillager.

Polymarket customers lose $3 million in supply-chain attack — Should Read

Polymarket will reimburse users after a third-party frontend supply-chain compromise injected malicious JavaScript into its official site, tricking users into approving fraudulent transactions. Blockchain firms estimate about $3 million was stolen from fewer than 15 accounts and later swapped into roughly 1,893 ETH.

  • Polymarket says it will fully reimburse customers who lost an estimated $3 million after hackers injected a malicious script into the platform's frontend through a third-party vendor dependency.
  • During the attack, users were tricked into approving fraudulent transactions on the official Polymarket website, while Polymarket says its own servers and backend infrastructure were not impacted.
  • Blockchain security firm PeckShield said the campaign stole approximately $3 million worth of ParyonUSD from users, which was bridged from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped into about 1,893 Ether.
  • Visual analytics company Bubblemaps said the incident affected fewer than 15 accounts and published some affected accounts and wallets holding the stolen funds.
  • Polymarket is a cryptocurrency-based prediction market valued at $9 billion that handles billions of dollars in trading volume.

Time Travel Debugging with Codex — May Read

SpecterOps shows that giving Codex access to Time Travel Debugging traces via TTDObjectsPy helped it solve the FLARE-ON FlareAuthenticator reverse-engineering challenge faster and correctly by grounding analysis in real execution history instead of static decompiler guesses. The key takeaway is that queryable runtime traces can reduce speculative reasoning and false confidence when LLMs analyze obfuscated binaries.

  • The article describes using TTDObjectsPy to give Codex programmatic access to Time Travel Debugging traces during reverse engineering.
  • The target was the FLARE-ON 12 FlareAuthenticator challenge, a Windows Qt GUI program that accepts a 25-digit code and either rejects it or shows a flag.
  • In the session without TTD, Codex identified relevant UI and submit paths but ultimately produced an incorrect flag after 1:51:28.706 and 69,501,208 total logged tokens.
  • In the session with TTDObjectsPy, Codex used execution traces to identify a 64-bit state comparison, extract digit contributions, solve the constraint, and recover the correct passcode and flag.
  • The article concludes that queryable execution history can help keep LLM-assisted reverse engineering grounded in program behavior and reduce speculative detours when behavior is runtime-dependent.

Open NDR and Threat Intelligence for Modern Detection | Corelight — Optional

Corelight is promoting its open network detection and response platform, positioning it as a way to combine network evidence with threat intelligence for stronger modern threat detection.

  • The article is titled “Open NDR and Threat Intelligence for Modern Detection | Corelight.”
  • The article names Corelight as the organization associated with the content.
  • The title indicates that the article discusses open network detection and response and threat intelligence in the context of modern detection.

item.title: Splunk CVE-2026-20253 Exploitation, Immobiliare npm Compromise, and Fake OpenAI Workspace Invites
item.content:encoded:
Critical Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in Splunk Enterprise (CVE-2026-20253) — Must Read

Splunk Enterprise has a critical unauthenticated RCE flaw, CVE-2026-20253, that is being actively exploited and can let attackers abuse exposed PostgreSQL recovery endpoints through Splunk Web to write files and run code as the Splunk service account. Organizations should urgently upgrade affected 10.2.x and 10.0.x versions, restrict Splunk Web exposure, or temporarily disable the PostgreSQL sidecar service where safe.

  • Splunk disclosed CVE-2026-20253 on June 10, 2026 as a critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise with a CVSS score of 9.8.
  • The vulnerability affects Splunk Enterprise 10.2.x prior to 10.2.4 and 10.0.x prior to 10.0.7, while Splunk Enterprise 9.4 and earlier and Splunk Cloud Platform are not affected.
  • Splunk updated its advisory on June 18, 2026 to warn of active exploitation, and CISA added CVE-2026-20253 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog the same day.
  • The flaw allows an unauthenticated attacker to reach PostgreSQL sidecar recovery endpoints through Splunk Web, create or write attacker-controlled files, and ultimately execute code as the Splunk service account.
  • Recommended actions include upgrading to a fixed release, temporarily disabling the PostgreSQL sidecar service when compatible with the instance’s features, and restricting network access to the Splunk Web management interface.

Chinese-Speaking APT Deploys New TinyRCT Backdoor in Southeast Asia Campaign — Must Read

A Chinese-speaking APT tracked as CL-STA-1062 is targeting Southeast Asian government and critical infrastructure organizations with a new custom .NET backdoor called TinyRCT. The group combines web shells and open-source tools like SoftEther VPN, Mimikatz, and VNT with TinyRCT to conduct reconnaissance, lateral movement, command execution, file theft, and screenshot capture.

  • Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 linked a Chinese-speaking APT actor tracked as CL-STA-1062 to attacks on government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia.
  • Unit 42 said CL-STA-1062 shares overlaps with UAT-7237 and has targeted strategic sectors in East Asia since March 2022.
  • The group uses a hybrid toolkit that includes open-source tools such as SoftEther VPN, Mimikatz, Yuze, and VNT, as well as a new custom backdoor called TinyRCT.
  • TinyRCT can run arbitrary commands, enumerate and exfiltrate files, capture the device screen, upload files, support remote control, and delete itself from the compromised host.
  • In one September 2025 campaign, the threat actor infiltrated a Southeast Asian government entity, deployed a web shell to exfiltrate data from an MS SQL server, and conducted network reconnaissance against another government entity in the same country.

Multiple @immobiliarelabs Backstage Plugins Compromised on npm — Must Read

Multiple Immobiliare Labs Backstage npm plugins were compromised with malicious patch releases that execute during npm install via a binding.gyp node-gyp hook. The payload steals a wide range of developer, cloud, CI/CD, and registry credentials and attempts persistence through AI coding assistant configurations.

  • On June 26, 2026, multiple versions of four Immobiliare Labs Backstage npm packages were found to contain a malicious payload that executes during npm install via a binding.gyp node-gyp hook.
  • The affected packages are @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-gitlab, @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-gitlab-backend, @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-ldap-auth, and @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-ldap-auth-backend.
  • Each compromised version includes a 5 MB index.js file and a binding.gyp file that were absent from prior releases, and all compromised versions were published within a 30-second window on June 26, 2026.
  • The payload targets credentials from sources including GitHub Actions, cloud provider services, Kubernetes, Vault, package registries, password managers, and SSH keys.
  • The payload includes an infectHost function that modifies configuration files for several AI coding assistants, including Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, VS Code, Aider, Amazon Kiro, Sourcegraph Cody, Google Gemini, and OpenAI Codex.

Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites — Should Read

Attackers are creating fake OpenAI/ChatGPT organizations that impersonate real companies and sending legitimate-looking OpenAI invites to employees. The goal appears to be tricking staff into using the attacker-controlled workspace and exposing sensitive company data in prompts, chats, or projects.

  • Threat actors are creating OpenAI tenants that impersonate legitimate companies and inviting employees to join them.
  • Push Security discovered the campaign after multiple employees received legitimate OpenAI invitations to an organization named “Push Security Inc.” that had been created by an attacker using Gmail addresses.
  • The invitation emails came from OpenAI’s legitimate notification address, passed email authentication checks, and looked like normal ChatGPT workspace invitations.
  • After accepting an invitation, a Push Security researcher found a fraudulent organization containing an attacker-controlled Gmail account posing as the company’s CEO and a Visa credit card attached to billing.
  • Push Security believes the attackers aim to trick employees into using the fake ChatGPT workspace so they submit sensitive information such as source code, internal documents, customer data, security research, or strategic plans.

Your First GRC Agent: A Red Teamer's Walkthrough — Optional

The article argues that agentic AI can make GRC more continuous and evidence-driven by monitoring controls, detecting drift, and creating findings automatically while preserving human approval for high-impact decisions. Its main advice is to start with low-risk, repetitive GRC tasks and require strong execution logs, least privilege, and human sign-off to build trust.

  • The article says agentic GRC systems differ from traditional automation because they can act when conditions are met, use current program context, and execute multiple steps such as analyzing, deciding, and acting in sequence.
  • The author argues that AI in GRC should support rather than replace human judgment, with models used for reasoning, summarization, and orchestration while humans define controls, thresholds, and policy decisions.
  • The article describes an example agent in Anecdotes Agent Studio that checks whether MFA evidence for ISO 27001:2022 control A.8.5 is older than 24 hours, queries an identity provider such as Okta or Entra ID, compares the policy against a baseline, and opens a finding if a group is out of enforcement.
  • The article says each agent run should produce an execution log showing the trigger, data read, comparison performed, decision reached, action taken, and evidence touched so that decisions can be reconstructed and reviewed.
  • The author recommends starting with high-toil, low-judgment tasks such as evidence gap detection, extracting findings from audit reports, or generating analysis rules before expanding agentic GRC use to higher-stakes areas.

Rolldown Pulls Rust React Compiler Integration After Binary Size Increase — Optional

Rolldown and Vite removed a Rust-based React Compiler integration because it increased the default binary size by about 5MB, which maintainers said was too much for a framework-agnostic tool used by many non-React projects. The team is exploring smaller and more opt-in approaches that could still bring React users native compiler speedups without making all users carry the cost.

  • Rolldown and Vite withdrew a Rust-based React Compiler integration after maintainers decided the increase from 28.7MB to 33.8MB, or 17%, was too costly for a feature shipped to all users by default.
  • The integration originated from Oxc work intended to let React apps avoid Babel pipelines when they only needed Babel for React Compiler, and it was later wired into Rolldown behind a new transform.reactCompiler option before the Rolldown PR was closed on June 9.
  • Critics argued that React-specific compiler support should not add weight to Vite or Rolldown core because Vite is framework-agnostic and used across many frameworks.
  • Evan You said Vite cannot give special treatment to React without raising the same question for Vue, Svelte, Solid, Astro, and other frameworks, especially given Vite’s 12 million weekly downloads.
  • Boshen is experimenting with a smaller implementation that he said shows up to a 2x performance improvement with only a 1.4MB binary size increase, but the code still needs to be made maintainable and upstreamed.

item.title: Turla StockStay, Windchill RCE, Linux pedit COW/DirtyClone, and Miasma Supply Chain Attacks
item.content:encoded:
CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue — Must Read

CISA added the critical PTC Windchill and FlexPLM RCE flaw CVE-2026-12569 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after attackers began actively using it to deploy JSP web shells. Organizations should patch immediately and check for the listed indicators of compromise, especially suspicious JSP files under the Windchill login path.

  • CISA added CVE-2026-12569, a critical remote code execution vulnerability affecting PTC Windchill PDMlink and PTC FlexPLM, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog due to evidence of active exploitation.
  • PTC described CVE-2026-12569 as an improper input validation issue that may be exploited through deserialization of untrusted data to execute arbitrary code.
  • PTC said that, despite patches being released last week, it continued to receive reports of heightened threat activity as of June 25.
  • Unknown attackers are exploiting the vulnerability to deploy JSP web shells against susceptible systems.
  • PTC published indicators of compromise and mitigation steps, including blocking 5.180.41.35, checking for suspicious JSP files under /Windchill/login/, and adding a rule to block requests containing the X-windchill-req header.

New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries — Must Read

A Linux kernel flaw dubbed pedit COW can let local unprivileged users gain root by poisoning cached setuid binaries in memory, with public exploit code already available. Patch and reboot affected systems, or mitigate by disabling act_pedit or unprivileged user namespaces where feasible.

  • CVE-2026-46331, nicknamed pedit COW, is an out-of-bounds write in the Linux kernel act_pedit component that can let a local unprivileged user gain root on affected systems.
  • A public working exploit appeared within a day of the CVE assignment on June 16 and poisons the cached in-memory copy of the setuid root binary /bin/su without modifying the file on disk.
  • The exploit requires act_pedit to be loadable and unprivileged user namespaces to be available, giving the attacker namespace-local CAP_NET_ADMIN needed to trigger the bug.
  • The PoC author reported successful unprivileged-to-root exploitation on RHEL 10 and Debian 13, while Ubuntu 24.04 required routing execution through permissive AppArmor profiles and Ubuntu 26.04 blocks that path by default.
  • The article recommends installing a patched kernel and rebooting, or mitigating by blocking act_pedit from loading or disabling unprivileged user namespaces if patching is not yet possible.

Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

The Miasma/Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain campaign has compromised new npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and a Go-related repository to steal developer credentials and propagate through trusted software ecosystems. The key risk is that the malware targets developer workflows broadly, using install-time execution, CI/CD secret theft, and GitHub dead drops to spread beyond a single package registry.

  • Cybersecurity researchers reported a new wave of activity linked to the Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware family affecting npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and a Go module associated with the Verana Blockchain project.
  • Socket said the campaign’s goal is to harvest developer or maintainer credentials and use the stolen data to spread across package registries, repositories, and trusted developer workflows.
  • The affected npm packages include LeoPlatform and RStreams packages, and researchers suspect an npm developer account associated with LeoPlatform maintainer czirker was breached to push trojanized versions.
  • The malicious npm packages use a binding.gyp file to execute code during installation, launch a JavaScript loader, install the Bun runtime if needed, and run a stealer payload for secrets, credentials, and tokens.
  • The malware also drops a GitHub Actions workflow named “Run Copilot” to capture CI/CD environment secrets and uploads stolen information to public GitHub repositories with the description “Alright Lets See If This Works.”

New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets — Should Read

DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503) is a Linux kernel flaw that can let local users gain root by corrupting file-backed memory through cloned network packets, especially on systems allowing unprivileged user namespaces. Administrators should install patched kernels or temporarily restrict user namespaces and affected networking modules.

  • DirtyClone, tracked as CVE-2026-43503 with a CVSS score of 8.8, lets a local user corrupt file-backed memory through a cloned network packet and gain root on affected Linux systems.
  • Exploitation requires CAP_NET_ADMIN to configure a loopback IPsec tunnel, and unprivileged user namespaces enabled by default on Debian and Fedora can allow a local user to obtain that capability inside a new namespace.
  • Ubuntu 24.04 and later restrict namespace creation via AppArmor, blocking the default exploit path described in the article.
  • The affected systems include multi-tenant servers, CI runners, container hosts, and Kubernetes clusters where untrusted users can create namespaces.
  • The fix landed upstream in Linux v7.1-rc5 and has been backported to stable and LTS branches, with advisories published by Ubuntu, Debian, and SUSE and a Red Hat Bugzilla tracking entry.

Turla group adds more malware to Russia’s espionage efforts against Ukraine — Should Read

Google researchers say Russia-linked Turla has been developing and using a malware strain called StockStay since at least 2022 to spy mainly on Ukrainian government and defense targets. The tool appears designed as a parallel replacement or backup to Turla’s older Kazuar malware, helping the group maintain persistent access even when other tools are detected.

  • Google researchers said the Russian state-backed Turla group has been developing and deploying a malware strain called StockStay since at least December 2022.
  • StockStay was primarily used to target Ukrainian government and defense organizations, with early samples also identified in Italy, the Netherlands, Poland, and Germany.
  • Google said StockStay shares significant code and functionality with Kazuar, another Turla malware framework previously used against military and defense targets in Ukraine.
  • Victims were typically infected through phishing emails containing malicious Remote Desktop Protocol configuration files that connected compromised computers to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
  • Researchers said Turla used academic and diplomatic themes in phishing campaigns, including emails from a compromised Ukrainian university account and abuse of a diplomatic education platform.

Russia used social engineering to breach prominent messaging accounts, Ukraine says — Should Read

Ukraine’s security agency says Russia ran a long-term social engineering campaign, uncovered with the FBI, to hijack messaging accounts of officials, military personnel, politicians and activists in Ukraine, Europe and the U.S. The attackers impersonated platform support staff to steal credentials or verification codes rather than exploiting flaws in apps like Signal, WhatsApp or Telegram.

  • Ukraine's Security Service said it uncovered, together with the FBI, a long-running Russian campaign to compromise messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians and activists in Ukraine, Europe and the United States.
  • The SBU said the campaign aimed to access sensitive military, political and economic information exchanged through messaging applications and to steal victims' personal data.
  • The attackers used social engineering techniques, including text messages impersonating official messaging platform support services and urging users to disclose account credentials.
  • The SBU said the messages were sent in the morning hours, when users were particularly vulnerable due to their physical and emotional state.
  • The SBU did not identify the Russian intelligence service responsible, specify which messaging platforms were primarily targeted or say how many victims had been affected.

FCC votes to toughen rules in bid to better protect undersea cables — Should Read

The FCC voted to tighten oversight of undersea internet cables by banning equipment from China and other foreign adversaries, adding licensing for key cable terminal equipment, and speeding approvals for trusted U.S.-based operators. The move aims to protect critical global internet infrastructure from spying, cyber disruption, and physical sabotage while supporting faster cable expansion.

  • The FCC voted to strengthen oversight of undersea submarine cables that carry nearly all internet traffic.
  • The new rules will block Chinese firms and companies from other countries deemed foreign adversaries from selling equipment related to undersea cables.
  • The FCC plans to require owners and operators of submarine line terminal equipment, which connects undersea cables to U.S. terrestrial facilities, to be licensed.
  • Cable operators that certify high security standards, have operated cables without incident, and accept ongoing oversight may avoid intensive approval reviews.
  • FCC Chair Brendan Carr said submarine cables face growing risks from cyber and physical disruptions and from bad actors seeking access to sensitive data and communications.

Russia accuses Apple of ‘political censorship’ after VK apps removed from App Store — May Read

Russia accused Apple of political censorship after the company removed major VK apps from the App Store, with Apple reportedly saying the removals were required to comply with sanctions. The move affects downloads and updates for VK services on Apple devices, though already installed apps continue to work.

  • Apple removed VKontakte, VK Music, VK Messenger, VK Video, Odnoklassniki, and Mail.ru services from its App Store, making them unavailable for download or updates on Apple devices.
  • Apple told BBC News Russia that it removed the applications to comply with sanctions regulations and follows the laws of the jurisdictions in which it operates, but did not specify which sanctions applied.
  • VK said Apple removed the apps without prior notice or explanation and called the decision unmotivated and unacceptable, arguing that the company itself had never been placed under sanctions.
  • Russian officials criticized the removal, with Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova calling it an act of political censorship and Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov questioning Apple's reliability.
  • VK said already installed apps would continue to function, while Android users could still download the services through RuStore, Google Play, Huawei AppGallery, Samsung Store, Xiaomi Store, and VK's official websites.

A Construction Bid Invitation Hid a Compromised Website Behind a Legitimate-Looking PDF Label — May Read

A phishing email disguised as a construction bid invitation used legitimate-looking PDF link text while hiding a URL to a compromised Brazilian website. Standard email authentication passed, so the key detection signal was the mismatch between the displayed document label and the actual link destination.

  • A phishing email formatted as a construction bid invitation was sent via blind carbon copy, with one named recipient used as the envelope address.
  • The visible link text was formatted as a bid-preview PDF filename, but the actual URL pointed to a path on a compromised Brazilian website unrelated to construction, the sender, or the recipient’s industry.
  • SPF, DKIM, and ARC all passed, the sender domain had no flagged reputation, and Microsoft’s spam confidence level was set to 1.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message as a suspicious link based on the mismatch between the link’s display text and its actual destination.
  • The article states that link-text consistency is an independent detection signal from link reputation because a clean-reputation domain can still be used in a phishing link.

Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance — Optional

AI agents are rapidly spreading through enterprises while inheriting human and service-account permissions that traditional IAM tools cannot monitor once actions begin. The article argues organizations need “guardian agents” to continuously discover, baseline, and enforce least privilege for autonomous AI activity at runtime.

  • AI agents in enterprise environments can inherit permissions from human or service identities and operate across multiple systems without the governance checkpoints used for traditional human access.
  • Traditional IAM, IGA, PAM, and CIEM tools are described as insufficient for agentic workloads because they focus on provisioning, authentication, vaulting, or platform-specific entitlements rather than runtime behavior across applications.
  • The article defines guardian agents as a control layer that continuously discovers AI agents, maps them to owners and inherited permissions, builds behavioral baselines, detects anomalies, and enforces least privilege during execution.
  • Unmanaged AI agents can create risks including over-privileged identities, orphaned sessions, stale credentials, prompt injection, and lateral movement through chained agent calls.
  • The article says organizations should bring AI agents under governance by discovering what is running, classifying agents by trust level and permission scope, enforcing least privilege at runtime, and integrating agent identity data with existing IAM and IGA systems.

Healthcare Cybersecurity in an Era of Geopolitical Instability — Optional

Healthcare organizations, especially in Europe, can no longer assume they are outside the scope of geopolitical cyber conflict. They need to factor geopolitical risk into governance, supply chains, third-party dependencies, and resilience planning.

  • Healthcare has traditionally been viewed as a protected, civilian, neutral, and essential sector, but that assumption is increasingly being tested.
  • The current geopolitical environment is marked by sustained instability, multiple military conflicts, and rising tensions across regions.
  • Cyber operations have become a persistent instrument of statecraft, and these developments are interconnected with geopolitical instability.
  • The article says healthcare is no longer outside the scope of geopolitical cyber operations.
  • European healthcare organisations must consider how geopolitical risk influences their threat exposure, supply chains, and dependencies on external providers.

item.title: Turla STOCKSTAY Targets Ukraine as Cellebrite Russia Use and Packagist Malware Blocking Emerge
item.content:encoded:
Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks — Must Read

Google says Russian state-backed Turla is using a newly documented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY for espionage against Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to European foreign policy. The malware overlaps with Turla’s Kazuar toolkit and has been delivered through phishing, malicious RDP files, MSI installers, and WinRAR exploit chains.

  • Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a previously undocumented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY to the Russian state-sponsored threat actor Turla.
  • STOCKSTAY has been deployed against government and military organizations in Ukraine and entities with an interest in Italian foreign policy.
  • The malware consists of multiple components, including STOCKSTAY.MARKETMAKER, STOCKSTAY.STOCKBROKER, STOCKSTAY.STOCKTRADER, and STOCKSTAY.STOCKMARKET, which communicate via Windows inter-process messages and a secure WebSocket connection.
  • STOCKSTAY.STOCKTRADER supports commands for file deletion and upload, directory enumeration, screen capture, process execution, Windows Registry operations, system information gathering, and ZIP extraction.
  • Google said STOCKSTAY shares significant code, functional, and architectural overlaps with Turla’s Kazuar backdoor, raising the possibility that both tools were developed and maintained in part by the same developer or team.

Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff — Should Read

Citizen Lab found that Russian authorities used Cellebrite UFED tools to extract data from jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov’s seized iPhone in June 2021, months after Cellebrite said it had stopped sales to Russia. The case shows that sales cutoffs can be ineffective when older offline forensic hardware remains usable by government agencies.

  • Citizen Lab reported that Russian authorities used Cellebrite UFED forensic tools to access detained opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov’s iPhone in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it would stop selling tools and services to Russia and Belarus.
  • Pivovarov’s iPhone 12 and MacBook were confiscated when he was pulled off a flight at St. Petersburg airport on May 31, 2021, and he did not consent to a search or provide passwords.
  • MobileLockdown records on the iPhone showed a June 17, 2021 connection to a host ID matching a Cellebrite fingerprint, which Citizen Lab assessed as high-confidence evidence of UFED use.
  • A Russian forensic report given to Pivovarov named Cellebrite’s UFED Physical Analyzer and UFED 4PC and documented searches for Open Russia Civic Movement and named opposition figures.
  • Cellebrite told Citizen Lab and Access Now that any use of its legacy hardware in Russia after March 2021 was entirely unauthorized and said Russia remains on its restricted-customer list.

Packagist is now protected by Aikido Intel and other updates to the PHP registry — Should Read

Packagist and Composer are adding default supply-chain protections for PHP, including Aikido Intel-powered blocking of known malicious package versions in Composer 2.10 and mandatory stable version immutability to stop re-tagging attacks. PHP developers should update Composer to benefit from these protections.

  • Aikido’s Intel feed now powers malware blocking in Composer downloads and is enabled by default for Composer 2.10 and higher.
  • Composer uses flagged package metadata to remove malicious package versions from dependency resolution for commands such as composer update, composer require, and composer create-project, and composer install fails if a locked version is later flagged.
  • Packagist introduced stable version immutability so a published stable version can no longer be rewritten, preventing re-tagging attacks like the one described against Laravel-Lang.
  • Private Packagist can require a current Composer client to connect and can refuse to serve flagged version files to any client, including older Composer versions.
  • Packagist plans additional security improvements including minimum release age, organizational ownership for mandatory MFA, and staged releases.
